m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to handle exceptions in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'try' keyword is used to start a block of code that will be tested for exceptions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which block is used to handle exceptions thrown by the try block in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'catch' block is used to handle exceptions thrown by the 'try' block.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the superclass of all exceptions in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: All exceptions in Java are derived from the 'Throwable' class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an unchecked exception in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: NullPointerException is an unchecked exception as it is not required to be caught or declared thrown.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When does the 'finally' block execute?

Options.OptionText: Only when an exception is thrown
Only when no exception is thrown
Always, regardless of whether an exception is thrown

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'finally' block executes whether or not an exception is thrown.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do you define your own exception in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: To define a custom exception, extend the Exception class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'throw' keyword?

Options.OptionText: To execute code after an exception

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'throw' keyword is used to manually throw an exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a type of exception in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: There is no such classification as 'Thrown exceptions' in Java. Exceptions are classified as checked and unchecked.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which exception will be thrown if a division by zero occurs?
m_Item1.Explanation: ArithmeticException is thrown when an exceptional arithmetic condition occurs, such as division by zero.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which is the parent class of the IOException class?
m_Item1.Explanation: IOException is a subclass of Exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a method does not handle a checked exception, what must it do to avoid a compilation error?

Options.OptionText: It must declare the exception using the 'throws' keyword

m_Item1.Explanation: If a method does not handle a checked exception, it must declare the exception using the 'throws' keyword.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the return type of a method that doesn't need to return a value if it throws an exception?
m_Item1.Explanation: A method can have any return type even if it throws an exception. The 'throw' will cause an immediate exit from the method.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a try block exist without a catch block?

Options.OptionText: Yes, if there is a finally block
No, a try block must have a catch block

m_Item1.Explanation: A try block can exist without a catch block if there is a finally block.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if an exception is thrown by the try block and there's no catch block?

Options.OptionText: The finally block executes and then the exception is propagated
The exception is automatically caught by the JVM

m_Item1.Explanation: The finally block executes first and then the exception propagates up the call stack.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If multiple catch blocks are present, how does Java determine which one to execute?

Options.OptionText: The last matching catch block executes
The JVM randomly picks a catch block
The first matching catch block executes

m_Item1.Explanation: Java executes the first catch block that matches the type of thrown exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What keyword is used to declare an exception that may be thrown by a method in its method signature?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'throws' keyword is used in a method signature to indicate that an exception may be thrown.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these will be executed regardless of whether an exception is thrown?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'finally' block will be executed regardless of whether an exception is thrown.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which type of exceptions are NOT checked by the compiler at compile-time?
m_Item1.Explanation: Unchecked exceptions are not checked by the compiler at compile-time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the correct way to declare a method that throws an IOException?
m_Item1.Explanation: The correct way to declare a method that throws an exception is to use 'throws' followed by the exception class name.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a single catch block handle multiple exception types?

Options.OptionText: No, you must have separate catch blocks
Yes, but only for unchecked exceptions
No, Java does not support this

m_Item1.Explanation: Java supports multi-catch syntax where a single catch block can handle multiple exception types.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if an exception is not caught in a program?

Options.OptionText: The program will ignore the exception
The program will execute the finally block
The program will handle the exception silently

m_Item1.Explanation: If an exception is not caught, the program will terminate abnormally.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is an exception in programming?

Options.OptionText: An event occurs during execution that disrupts the normal flow of instructions

m_Item1.Explanation: An exception is an event that occurs during the execution of a program that disrupts the normal flow of instructions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not a valid Throwable type in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Java, 'Interrupt' is not a valid Throwable type. Throwable includes Error and Exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the use of a throw statement?

Options.OptionText: To manually trigger a specific exception

m_Item1.Explanation: The throw statement is used to manually trigger a specific exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output if an exception is caught by a catch block but there's an exception inside the catch block itself?

Options.OptionText: The finally block will be executed
The first exception is handled, but the catch block exception propagates

m_Item1.Explanation: If an exception is caught and there's an additional exception within the catch block, the first is handled, but the catch block exception propagates.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If we try to cast the classes out of hierarchy we get
m_Item1.Explanation: Write explaination here..

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which method is used to print the description of the exception?
m_Item1.Explanation: printStackTrace() is a widely used method for printing exception message in catch block

m_Item1.QuestionText:
int x = 50;
if(x == 20 && x/0 ==25)
   System.out.println("Yes");
else
  System.out.println("No");
What is the output of the above code?
m_Item1.Explanation: In if condition as && operator is used, control will not check for second condition as first condition itself has failed. So else part gets executed which prints "No" on the console.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the following code,
public class Test
{
        String str="hello";
}
1. Test t=new Test();
2. System.out.println(t.str);
3. t=null;
4. System.out.println(t.str);
5. System.out.println("done");
What will happen to the above code?
m_Item1.Explanation: t=null so line 4 gives error for Null pointer Exception

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a checked exception?
m_Item1.Explanation: InterruptedException extends from Exception and rest of the classes extend from RuntimeException. So InterruptedException is a checked exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If you write System.exit(0) at the end of try block, will the finally block still execute?
m_Item1.Explanation: Finally block does not get executed only in one condition i.e System.exit() call in try block

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given this code snippet: 
  try  { 
   tryThis(); 
   return; 
  }
  catch (IOException x1) { 
   System.out.println("exception 1"); 
   return;
  }
  catch (Exception x2)  { 
   System.out.println("exception 2"); 
   return;
  }
  finally  {  
   System.out.println("finally");
  }
public void tryThis() {
     throw new NullPointerException();
}
m_Item1.Explanation: NullPointerException can be caught by second (catch(Exception x2)) catch block. So it will print "exception 2" followed by "finally" in finally block. Finally block always gets executed inspite of having return statement in try and catch block.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What letters get written to the standard output with the following code? 
  class Unchecked   {  
   public static void main(String[] args)   { 
   try { 
   method();
   }
   catch (Exception e)    { 
   }
          } 
static void method() { 
  try   {  
  wrench(); 
  System.out.println("a");
  }
  catch (ArithmeticException e)   { 
          System.out.println("b");
  }
  finally   { 
          System.out.println("c");
  } 
  System.out.println("d");
}
static void wrench() { 
          throw new NullPointerException();
}
}
m_Item1.Explanation: There is no catch block which catches NullPointerException. So directly finally block gets executed which prints "c". Then it moves to empty catch block of main and terminates.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
class exception_handling {
         public static void main(String args[]) {
                  try {
                       System.out.print("Hello" + " " + 1 / 0);
                 }
                 catch(ArithmeticException e) {
                            System.out.print("World");
                 }
        }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: 1/0 raises ArithmeticException which will print "World". So print statement in the try block does not get executed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
try {
         int x=0; int y=50/x; 
        System.out.println("Division by zero"); 
}catch(ArithmeticException e) {
        System.out.println("catch block");
}
What will be the output?
m_Item1.Explanation: When exception is raised then control will come out from try block and search for matching catch block. Remaining statements in the try block does not get executed. So it prints "catch block"

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
Float pi = new Float(3.14f);
if (pi > 3) {
System.out.print("pi is bigger than 3. ");
}
else {
System.out.print("pi is not bigger than 3. ");
}
finally {
System.out.println("Have a nice day.");
}
What is the result?

Options.OptionText: pi is not bigger than 3. Have a nice day

m_Item1.Explanation: Compilation falis because finally without try is not allowed .

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is true about Exception and Error class?

Options.OptionText: The Error class is final and Exception class is not.
The Exception class is final and the Error is not.

m_Item1.Explanation: Exception and Error extends from Throwable which is a class and none of the class is declared as final.

m_Item1.QuestionText: _____________ is caused when a conversion between string and number fails.
m_Item1.Explanation: NumberFormatException is raised when string can not be converted into number properly.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
public static void parse(String str) {
try {
float f = Float.parseFloat(str);
}
catch (NumberFormatException nfe) {
f = 0;
}
finally {
System.out.println(f);
}
}
public static void main(String[] args) {
parse("invalid");
}
What is the result?

Options.OptionText: A ParseException is thrown by the parse method at runtime.
A NumberFormatException is thrown by the parse method at runtime.

m_Item1.Explanation: Variable f is not accessible in catch block. So it raises compilatiion error.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
public class Foo {
   static int[] a;
   static {
​​​​​​​          a[0]=2;
   }
   public static void main( String[] args ) {
   }
}
Which exception or error will be thrown when a programmer attempts to run this code?
m_Item1.Explanation: An ExceptionInInitializerError is thrown to indicate exception occurred inside a static initializer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these statements is true?

Options.OptionText: For each try block there must be at least one catch block defined.
A try block may be followed by any number of finally blocks.
A try block must be followed by at least one catch or finally block.
If both catch and finally blocks are defined, finally block must precede the catch block.

m_Item1.Explanation: A try block can not exists alone. It has to be followed by atleast one catch block or one finally block. Finally block if exists should be written after catch block

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if an exception is not caught (or never handled)?

Options.OptionText: VM  is killed abruptly, with error code 1.
Exception  being safer than error. calling function automatically handles it

The uncaughtExceptionHandler's handleException() method of the thread's ThreadGroup being invoked. which eventually results in the termination of the thread in which it is thrown and will print the stack trace
In multithreaded application  Exceptions behaves just as Errors.

m_Item1.Explanation: Every thread belongs to ThreadGroup by default which has implemented UncaughtExceptionHandler and overrides handleException() method which prints stack trace of exception that has occured

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements are true about checked and unchecked  exceptions?

Options.OptionText: A checked exception is some subclass of Exception (or Exception itself). excluding class RuntimeException and its subclasses.
Unchecked exceptions are RuntimeException and any of its subclasses
Class error and its subclasses also are unchecked.

m_Item1.Explanation: All statements are true. Checked exceptions are Exception and its subclasses. Unchecked exceptions are RuntimeException and Error and their subclasses.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these keywords must be used to handle the exception thrown by try block in some rational manner?
m_Item1.Explanation:
The try statement allows you to define a block of code to be tested for errors while it is being executed.
The catch statement allows you to define a block of code to be executed, if an error occurs in the try block

m_Item1.QuestionText: All exceptions are derived from the
m_Item1.Explanation:
The java.lang.Throwable class is the superclass of all errors and exceptions in the Java language. Only objects that are instances of this class (or one of its subclasses) are thrown by the Java Virtual Machine or can be thrown by the Java throw statement.
Following is the declaration for java.lang.Throwable class −
public class Throwable
   extends Object
      implements Serializable

m_Item1.QuestionText:
public class Test{
     public static void main(String args[]){
         try {
             int i;
             return;
         }
         catch(Exception e) {
             System.out.print("inCatchBlock");
         }
         finally {
             System.out.println("inFinallyBlock");
         }
     }
}

Options.OptionText: The program will return without printing anything

m_Item1.Explanation: Exception does not occur in try block so that control directly goes to finally block

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given this code snippet: 
  try  { 
     tryThis(); 
     return; 
  }
  catch (IOException x1) { 
     System.out.println("exception 1"); 
     return;
  }
  catch (Exception x2) { 
     System.out.println("exception 2"); 
     return;

     System.out.println("finally");
  }

Options.OptionText: It depends on how tryThis() method is implemented

m_Item1.Explanation: Outcome depends upon how tryThis() method is written, which exception method throws

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the program?(Consider no arguments are passed)
public class Test
{
       public static void main (String[] args)
       {
             String foo = args[1];
             String bar = args[2];
             String baz = args[3];
             System.out.println("baz = " + baz); /* Line 8 */
       }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: This will generate ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException for args[1] because no arguments are passed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true ?

Options.OptionText: A finally block is executed before the catch block but after the try block.
A finally block is executed, only after the catch block is executed.
A finally block is executed whether an exception is thrown or not.
A finally block is executed, only if an exception occurs.

m_Item1.Explanation: <font color="#0000ff">if try, catch and finally block exists, finally should be written after catch block. So finally block gets executed after catch block if exists or after try block. Finally block executes even if there is exception or no exception.</font>

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these operator is used to generate an instance of an exception than can be thrown by using throw?
m_Item1.Explanation: new is used to create instance of an exception. All of java’s built in run-time exceptions have two constructors : one with no parameters and one that takes a string parameter.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these keywords is used by the function to guard against the exception that is thrown but not handled within the function?
m_Item1.Explanation: If a method is capable of causing an exception that it does not handle. It must specify this behaviour the behaviour so that callers of the method can guard themselves against that exception. This is done by using throws clause in methods declaration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Incorrect URL throws an exception called
m_Item1.Explanation: MalformedURLException exception thrown to indicate when illegal URL called.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which  of these class  is related to all the checked exceptions  that can  be caught by using catch?
m_Item1.Explanation: Exception is the super class for all checked exceptions.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following are the most common run-time errors in Java programming?
i) Missing semicolons
ii) Dividing an integer by zero
iii) Converting invalid string to number
iv) Missing import statement
m_Item1.Explanation: Dividing by zero and converting non-numeric string into number are run time errors. Missing semicolon and bad reference of objects can be recognized at compile time.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output for the below code ?
import java.io.FileNotFoundException;
class A {
      public void printName() throws FileNotFoundException{
            System.out.println("Value-A");
      }
}
class B extends A {
      public void printName() throws NullPointerException{
            System.out.println("Name-B");
      }
}
public class Test {
      public static void main (String[] args) throws Exception{
            A a = new B();
            a.printName();
      }
}

Options.OptionText: Compilation fails-Exception NullPointerException is not compatible with throws clause in A.printName()

m_Item1.Explanation: The overriding method can throw any unchecked (runtime) exception, regardless of exception thrown by super class method. NullPointerException is RuntimeException so compiler will not complain.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these keywords is used to manually throw an exception?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Java throw keyword is used  to explicitly throw an exception. We can throw either checked or uncheked exception in java by throw keyword. The throw keyword is mainly used to throw custom exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
public class ExceptionDemo {
public static void main(String [] args) {
System.out.println("method return -> "+m());
}
static String m() {
try {
int i = 10/0;
}
catch(ArithmeticException e) {
return "catch";
}
finally {
return "finally";
}
}
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Method m() returns "finally" because finally block always gets executed and method can not return 2 values. So it prints "method returns -> finally"

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Predict the output of the following program.
class Test {
        String str = "a";
        void A() {
            try {
                   str +="b";
                   B();
             }
             catch (Exception e) {
                  str += "c";
             }
         }
         void B() throws Exception {
             try {
                  str += "d";
                  C();
             }
             catch(Exception e) {
                    throw new Exception();
             }
             finally {
                  str += "e";
             }
             str += "f";
         }
         void C() throws Exception {
                  throw new Exception();
         }
         void display() {
                 System.out.println(str);
         }
         public static void main(String[] args) {
               Test object = new Test();
               object.A();
               object.display();
         }
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
A method appends "b" to "a" , then B method appends "d" to str, then finally block appends "e" and then catch block of A mehod appends "c".
Call to method C() throws an exception. Thus, control goes in catch block of method B() which again throws an exception. So, control goes in catch block of method A().

m_Item1.QuestionText:
class Test
{
      int count = 0;
      void A() throws Exception {
            try {
                count++;
                try {
                     count++;
                     try {
                         count++;
                         throw new Exception();
                     }
                    catch(Exception ex) {
                          count++;
                         throw new Exception();
                    }
               }
             catch(Exception ex) {
                    count++;
             }
          }
          catch(Exception ex) {
               count++;
          }
       }
      void display() {
            System.out.println(count);
      }
       public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception  {
                 Test obj = new Test();
                 obj.A();
                 obj.display();
       }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: ‘throw’ keyword is used to explicitly throw an exception. In third try block, exception is thrown. So, control goes in catch block.Again, in catch block exception is thrown. So, control goes in inner catch block.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
State whether the following statements are True or False.
i) A catch can have comma-separated multiple arguments.
ii) Throwing an Exception always causes program termination.
m_Item1.Explanation:
Catch block can not have comma seperated multiple arguments. At the max catch block can have multiple exceptions but they are pipe seperated.
e.g catch(ArithmeticException|NumberFormatException e)
When exception is handled in catch block, it will continue executing the statement after the try-catch block

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
//some code here line 31
try {
//some code here line 33
} catch (NullPointerException e1) {
//some code here line 35
} finally {
//some code here line 37
}
Under which the code on line 37 will not be executed?

Options.OptionText: The code on line 33 throws an exception.
The code on line 35 throws an exception.
The code on line 31 throws an exception.
The code on line 33 executes successfully.

m_Item1.Explanation: Finally block always gets executed even if exception occurs or not in the try block. Only when exception occurs before try block, then control will not go to the finally block.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is known as the classes that extend Throwable class except RuntimeException and Error?
m_Item1.Explanation: Classes that extend from RuntimeException and Error are called unchecked exception and classes that extend from Throwable and Exception eventually are checked exceptions.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which  default exception handler that performs the following tasks:
- Prints out exception description.
- Prints the stack trace (Hierarchy of methods where the exception occurred).
- Causes the program to terminate.
m_Item1.Explanation: JVM is the default exception handler to take care of any exceptions not caught by the thread in the run() method. This is a method of the ThreadGroup class.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
. What is the output of this program?
    class Output {
        public static void main(String args[]) {    
             int x , y = 1;
             x = 10;
             if (x != 10 && x / 0 == 0)
                 System.out.println(y);
             else
                 System.out.println(++y);
        }
    }

Options.OptionText: Runtime error owing to division by zero in if condition

m_Item1.Explanation:
Operator short circuit and, &&, skips evaluating right hand operand if left hand operand is false thus division by zero in if condition does not give an error.
output:
$ javac Output.java
$ java Output
2

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the program?
public class Foo {  
   public static void main(String[] args) {
     try { 
       return; 
     } 
     finally {
       System.out.println( "Finally" ); 
     } 
   } 
}

Options.OptionText: The code runs with no output.
An exception is thrown at runtime.

m_Item1.Explanation: Finally block is always executed. So it prints "Finally".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the program?
    try {
           int x = 0;
            int y = 5 / x;
    }
    catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Exception");
    }
    catch (ArithmeticException ae) {
           System.out.println(" Arithmetic Exception");
    }
            System.out.println("finished");
m_Item1.Explanation: Here, in 1st catch block Exception is used and in 2nd catch block ArithmaticException is used which is subclass of Exception. It gives compile time error. More specific exceptions to be written before generalized exceptions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used when a method does not want to handle an exception?
m_Item1.Explanation: throws is a keyword which is used to give a warning to the caller method instead of handling the exception on it's own.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
static void test() throws RuntimeException {
try { 
System.out.print("test ");
throw new RuntimeException();
}
catch (Exception ex) {
System.out.print("exception ");
}
}
public static void main(String[] args) {
try {
test();
}
catch (RuntimeException ex) { 
System.out.print("runtime "); 
}
System.out.print("end ");
}
What is the result?
m_Item1.Explanation: RuntimeException can be caught in the catch block of Exception because Exception is super class of RuntimeException. As exception is already caught, catch block in the main method will not get executed. So it will print "test exception end".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What should replace XXXX ?
class MyException extends Exception { }
    public void method() throws XXXX {
       throw new MyException(); 
   }
m_Item1.Explanation: Here customized exception is created so class MyException extends Exception and at the time of throws we have to use MyException.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When does Exceptions in Java arises in code sequence?
m_Item1.Explanation: Exceptions are run time errors

m_Item1.QuestionText: Attempting to access a character that is outside the bounds of a StringBuffer results in a
m_Item1.Explanation: When trying to access index beyond range within string, it raises StringIndexOutOfBoundsException

m_Item1.QuestionText:
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            System.out.printf("1");
            int sum = 9 / 0;
            System.out.printf("2");
        }
        catch(ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.printf("3");
        }
        catch(Exception e) {
            System.out.printf("4");
        }
        finally {
            System.out.printf("5");
        }
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: When execution begins, in the try block "1" gets printed. After this statement ArithmeticException will be thrown from try block which will cause catch block for ArithmeticException to execute which prints "3". As finally block always gets executed, it will print "5". So final output will be "135"

m_Item1.QuestionText:
public static void main(String[] args) {
    Boolean flag = null;
    try {
    if (flag) {
        System.out.println("No exception");
    }
    } finally {
    System.out.println("Finally in finally");
    }
}

Options.OptionText: Finally in Finally and NullPointerException - stack trace

m_Item1.Explanation: As flag has not been assigned any value, it raises NullPointerException. As no catch block exists, finally block executes and default exception handler gets executed for NullPointerException

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which exception is thrown when java is out of memory?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Xms flag has no default value, and Xmx typically has a default value of 256MB. A common use for these flags is when you encounter a java.lang.OutOfMemoryError.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is legal?
m_Item1.Explanation: All syntaxes are legal

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be returned?
try{return 1;}
catch(Exception e){return 2;}
finally{return 3;}
m_Item1.Explanation: finally block will get executed returning 3 from the method.

m_Item1.QuestionText: One of the following is unchecked exception
m_Item1.Explanation: All exceptions are checked exceptions

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which one is checked exception
m_Item1.Explanation: MalformedUrlException is the checked exception because it extends from Exception

m_Item1.QuestionText: In order to declare exception which keyword is used
m_Item1.Explanation: throws keyword is used for declaring exception

m_Item1.QuestionText: Class.forName throws
m_Item1.Explanation: Write explaination here..

m_Item1.QuestionText: Checked exceptions are automatically propagated to the caller.
m_Item1.Explanation: An exception propagates from method to method, up the call stack, until it's caught. For checked exceptions throws keyword is required as method level warning.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Unchecked exceptions are automatically propagated to the caller.
m_Item1.Explanation: An exception propagates from method to method, up the call stack, until it's caught.For unchecked exception throws keyword is not required.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If u want to create checked exception as user defined exception u need to extend
m_Item1.Explanation: Base class should be Exception which creates checked exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
When u write one try and multiple catch the most specific catch should precede the most
generic catch
m_Item1.Explanation: When there are multiple catch blocks, most specific catch block should precede most generalized catch block.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given Following code:
    import java.io.*;
    class sub extends base {
            void disp() throws IOException {
                }   
    }
class base {
    void disp() throws Exception {
    }
  }
 public class myclass {
    public static void main(String args[]) {
        try {
            base b=new sub();
            b.disp();
        }
        catch(Exception ee) {
            System.out.println(ee);
        }
        System.out.println(“done”);
        }
    }
m_Item1.Explanation: No exception is thrown from the disp() method. so it directly executes the statement followed by that catch block

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is false from the following?

Options.OptionText: we can have try and finally without catch
finally gets executed irrespective whether exception is raised or not
if system.exit is called from within try or catch, finally will not be executed at all

m_Item1.Explanation: All statements are true, so option D is correct

m_Item1.QuestionText: Class.forName() requires which of the following exception to be handled
m_Item1.Explanation: Checked exception ClassNotFoundException is thrown by the method Class.forName().

m_Item1.QuestionText: Class.newInstance() requires which of the following exception to be handled
m_Item1.Explanation: newInstance() method throws checked exception IllegalAccessException.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Imagine there are two exception classes Exception1 and Exception2 derived from the Exception class.
    Given these two definitions:
    class First {
       void test()throws Exception1,Exception1 {
           }
    }
class Second extends First {
      void test() {
          }
    }
Now define a class “Third” derived from “Second” and override “test ()” method inside it.
What exceptions can Third’s test() method throw?

Options.OptionText: it can declare any checked exception

m_Item1.Explanation: When super class method does not throw any exception, subclass overridden method can throw any unchecked exception but can not throw any checked exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What letters get written to the standard output with the following code?
public class MyClass {
   public static void main(String args[]) {
      try {
        method();
     }
     catch(Exception ie) {
     }
  }
  static void method() {
      try {
         wrench();
         System.out.println("a");
      }
      catch(ArithmeticException ae) {
         System.out.println("b");
     }
     finally {
          System.out.println("c");
     }
    System.out.println("d");
  }
  static void wrench() {
     throw new NullPointerException();
  }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: When NullPointerException is thrown, it goes to finally block and prints "c". And then returns to main method.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is false from the following?

Options.OptionText: The exceptions that are checked at compilation-time by the Java Compiler are called ‘Checked exception’.
All exceptions are descendants of Throwable
The exceptions that are checked by the JVM are called ‘unchecked exception’

m_Item1.Explanation: All statement are correct

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Read the following code below.
public interface AQuestion {
    public abstract void someMethod() throws Exception;
}
A Class implementing this interface should

Options.OptionText: Should compulsorily have the method public abstract void someMethod();

Should have the method public void someMethod() which compulsorily has to throw an exception which is a
subclass of java.lang.Exception.
May have the method public void someMethod() which may throw an Exception or which may not throw an exception.

m_Item1.Explanation: When the method declares a throws in its declaration, it's overridden method may or may not throw any exception. But this overridden method should not throw any extra checked exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Write new question text hereGiven:
public class Test {
     public static void throwIt() {
         throw new Exception();
     }
     public static void main(String[] args) {
         try {
             System.out.println("Hey There");
         }
         finally {
             System.out.println("in Finally");
         }
     }
}
What will happen when one tries to compile and run above code?

Options.OptionText: The program will print Hey There, then will print in finally.

The program will print Hey There, then will print that an Exception has occurred, and then will
print in finally.

m_Item1.Explanation: throwit() method throws the Exception instance but does not handle it. As Exception is checked exception, it is compulsory to handle, so will result in compilation error

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
 public class Foo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
       try {
          return;
       } finally {
          System.out.println( "Finally" );
       }
    }
 }
What is the result?
m_Item1.Explanation: If explicit return statemenet is written in try block, still it executes finally block before returning

m_Item1.QuestionText:
In exception handling mechanism, finally block is always executed, even if no exception occurred in
the try block

Options.OptionText: Depends on the type of exception
Depends on the way catch block is written

m_Item1.Explanation: finally block is always executed

m_Item1.QuestionText: Exceptions can be caught or rethrown to a calling method.
m_Item1.Explanation: Exceptions can be caught or even rethrown

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given Following code:
import java.io.*;
class base {
   void disp() throws IOException {
   }
}
class sub extends base {
   void disp() throws Exception {
   }
}
public class myclass {
   public static void main(String args[]) {
   }
}

Options.OptionText: no compilation error but exception at runtime.

m_Item1.Explanation: Widening the scope of exception being thrown from subclass overridden method is not allowed. Base class method throws IOException and subclass method throws Exception which is widening the scope.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will happen to the following code?
public class Test {
     public static void aMethod() throws Exception {
     try /* Line 5 */
     {
               throw new Exception(); /* Line 7 */
     }
     finally /* Line 9 */
     {
            System.out.print("finally "); /* Line 11 */
     }
 }
 public static void main(String args[]) {
     try  {
        aMethod();
     }
     catch (Exception e) /* Line 20 */
     {
           System.out.print("exception ");
     }
         System.out.print("finished"); /* Line 24 */
     }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: When exception gets thrown from line 7, finally block gets executed printing "finally" then control goes to catch block at line 20 and prints "exception" and finally before it ends, it prints "finished".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which statement is true,if the following program is run by using command java test10
public class test10 {
    public static void main(String []args)  {
        String []num={"one","two","three","four"};
        if(args.length==0) {
            System.out.println("Zero");
        }
        else {
            System.out.println(num[args.length]+" arguments”);
        }
    }
}

Options.OptionText: The program won’t run because argument of main is not properly mentioned
The program will throw a NullPointerException
The program will display Zero when executed
The program will display 0 arguments when executed

m_Item1.Explanation: As no command line arguments are passed while executing the program, if condition becomes true and it prints "Zero".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Assuming a method contains code which may raise an Exception (but not a RuntimeException), what
is the correct way for a method to indicate that it expects the caller to handle that exception:
m_Item1.Explanation: When method does not want to deal with the exception, it can declare it using throws.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the result of executing the following code, using the parameters 4 and 0:
public void divide(int a, int b) {
   try {
      int c = a / b;
   }
   catch (Exception e) {
      System.out.print("Exception ");
    }
    finally {
       System.out.println("Finally");
   }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: As value of b is 0, it raises exception which is handled by catch block printing "Exception" followed by execution of finally block printing "Finally".
